quiz.title: Data Inferences and Arguments
quiz.subtitle: Unit 14 Quiz 10: Logic and Bias in Statistics
quiz.subject: Mathematics

questions.question: A researcher stands at the school entrance and surveys every 5th student who walks in. Which sampling method does this represent?
questions.explanation: Surveying every nth person is systematic random sampling. The researcher uses a fixed interval to select participants, giving each person an equal chance of being chosen without relying on volunteers or specific subgroups. This method produces a representative sample from which valid inferences can be drawn.

questions.question: A researcher wants to know if a new fertilizer helps plants grow. They give fertilizer to 50 plants and only water to another 50 plants. What type of study is this?
questions.explanation: Because a 'treatment' (fertilizer) is actively applied to one group to compare it against a control group, it is an experiment.

questions.question: A survey asks 900 randomly selected high school students about their study habits to draw conclusions about all 8,000 students in the district. Only 500 students participated in the survey. What is the population?
options: The 900 students selected for the survey
The 400 students who did not respond
questions.explanation: The population is the entire group about which you want to draw conclusions — all 8,000 students. The 900 selected is the intended sample; the 500 who participated is the actual sample.

questions.question: A news station invites viewers to call in and vote on a topic. Out of the station's 50,000 viewers, 1,200 people called in. 78% of callers voted "Yes." What is the main reason the results may not reflect all 50,000 viewers?
options: The sample is voluntary, so it likely overrepresents strong opinions
The sample size of 1,200 is far too small for a population of 50,000
At least, 90% of callers must vote Yes for the result to have any credibility
The 48,800 who didn't call all would have voted "No"
questions.explanation: Only 1,200 out of 50,000 chose to participate. This is a voluntary response sample, which tends to attract people with strong opinions. The 48,800 who didn't call may feel differently, so the 78% result is likely biased.

questions.question: If a sample of 100 students shows that 20% prefer pizza, how many students in a school of 1,000 would you infer prefer pizza?
questions.explanation: To make an inference, apply the sample percentage to the whole population: $0.20 \cdot 1,000 = 200$.

questions.question: Which of the following would lead to the most 'Generalizable' results?
questions.explanation: Generalizability increases with sample size and the use of randomness to eliminate bias.

questions.question: A principal surveys 100 students by only asking those waiting outside the honors classroom. The survey reports that 92% of students feel "challenged" by their courses. When the district later surveys all 1,200 students randomly, only 64% feel challenged. The difference between these results is best explained by:
options: Random error in the district survey
A statistical anomaly with no clear cause
Bias in the principal's sampling method
A constraint on the number of students surveyed
questions.explanation: The principal surveyed only honors students, a non-representative group. This sampling bias produced results (92%) that differed significantly from the true population parameter (64%). The systematic error came from how the sample was selected, not from random chance.

questions.question: An ice cream shop surveys only customers buying vanilla ice cream about their favorite flavor. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: By only asking vanilla customers, the shop is excluding people who prefer other flavors, leading to a biased result.

questions.question: To determine the effect of music on study habits, a teacher observes students in the library without interfering. This is a(n):
questions.explanation: Since the teacher is just watching and not assigning specific music or 'treatments,' it is an observational study.

questions.question: Which of these is most important for showing that one thing 'Causes' another?
options: A strong positive correlation on a scatter plot
A high frequency in a two-way table
questions.explanation: Correlation does not equal causation. Only a controlled experiment can help establish a cause-and-effect relationship.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You can think critically about how data is used and misused.
scoreMessages.70: Good job. Remember: a sample is only as good as the method used to pick it.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing. Focus on the difference between 'observing' and 'experimenting.'
scoreMessages.0: Review the concepts of Bias and Random Sampling.
